Nisreen Burhan ·
Codependency · Essay No. 01

Why Do Women
Feel Codependent?

Nisreen Burhan 8 min read April 2026

This is not a clinical paper. This is a letter — to you, to the version of yourself that has been giving and giving and wondering why it never feels like enough.

What is codependency — really?

For someone hearing this word for the first time — codependency means deriving all of your emotions, your sense of personal worth, your right to joy or to grief, from another person. Not from yourself. From them.

Many women may not have felt this consciously. But perhaps there are small signs, quiet signals, that whoever is reading this right now can begin to notice in themselves.

—

The rush toward someone else’s life

Every young woman has been through relationships and dating. And sometimes we find ourselves drawn to someone who carries the qualities we’ve always dreamed of. But why?

Yes, we deserve someone who shares our values. But often — driven by a desire to feel special, to hold a certain place in the world — we rush toward the most handsome, the most successful, the most generous. Someone who seems to carry everything we ever wanted, offered to us without us having to reach for it ourselves.

And if we look a little deeper, we find something rooted there: a distrust of our own capabilities. A quiet, persistent belief that we are not enough on our own. And that distrust is what drove us — sometimes desperately — to place our dreams, our sense of home, our vision of a full life, into the hands of someone else.

This is the centre of the problem. Not the man. Not the relationship. The belief — long before either existed — that she was not enough to build her own life.

I am not saying a woman shouldn’t build, work, and reach for everything she wants — she absolutely should. But there are social conditions, traditions, and long-held beliefs that have restricted that possibility for women for generations. And so the inner feeling of unworthiness found something to lean on. A reason to stay still.

The wound begins long before the relationship does. It begins in the room where she first learned what love felt like — and what it cost.
On childhood & the roots of codependency

Where did this begin? The first years.

It began, most often, in the first years of life. A child does not develop a genuine sense of self until after the first five years. During that time, the mother and the father are a direct mirror — the child sees themselves reflected in how they are treated, spoken to, held or ignored.

So they learn, very early, to read the room. To speak when the parent is in a good mood. To feel worthy only when a specific kind of praise arrives. To shrink when the environment becomes unsafe.

When a child grows up with a parent who is inconsistent, volatile, absent, or cruel — the nervous system adapts. It learns that love is not reliable. That safety is conditional. That the way to survive is to monitor the other person’s emotions, not your own. And they carry that into adulthood. Into every relationship. Into the way they love.

—

Why is this wound so much wider in women?

Because women are raised in environments that prescribe a very specific image: be obedient. Listen to men — they know better. Don’t dream too large. Be beautiful so that men will want you. Almost everything in the environment tells a woman to perform for someone else. To measure her worth by how well she is received.

The first wound: she lost her sense of self. The second: a society that built a cage around that emptiness and decorated it with expectations.

And so women place their unlived dreams onto one person. And when that man leaves, they fall into a grief with no visible bottom. Because he was the mirror. And without him, there is nothing left to reflect her back to herself.

—

Signs that a woman may be living in codependency

These are not judgments. They are recognitions. Read them slowly.

01

She is afraid to express her own desires — afraid of losing her place in someone’s eyes if she does.

02

She feels empty without a significant person — someone who gives her the feeling that her life has meaning and value.

03

A recurring inner voice: I can’t. I’m not ready. I’ll do it later. Always more time. Always later.

04

Her mood rises and falls entirely based on whoever she depends on. If they are upset, her world becomes unsafe.

05

She apologises constantly and over-explains — for things that are not her fault. For simply having needs.

06

She confuses love with fear. She believes she loves someone — and underneath is a terror of being left alone with herself.

Recognition is the beginning. Not the end. What you name, you can finally face.
On awareness & what comes after

So if you found yourself somewhere in what you just read — that recognition is real. And it matters.

But awareness alone is not enough. Knowing the name of something does not heal it. That is a different kind of work.

And you don’t have to do it alone.

If any of this felt familiar — I’m here.

Nisreen Burhan · Clinical Psychology

Who I work with

You might be
in the right
place if you…

Relationship Disconnection

You find yourself repeating the same painful patterns in relationships — pushing people away, over-attaching, or feeling alone even when someone is right beside you. Attachment wounds run deep, and they show up everywhere.

Chronic Anxiety

A persistent sense of worry, tension, or dread that doesn't go away — even when nothing is "wrong." Your nervous system is stuck in overdrive, and your mind can't find rest no matter how hard you try.

Loss of Meaning & Purpose

You've lost the thread of what used to matter. Work feels hollow, relationships feel distant, and the things that once brought you joy no longer reach you. This isn't laziness — it's a signal worth listening to.

Depression & Persistent Sadness

A heaviness that lives in the body as much as the mind. Low energy, emotional numbness, withdrawal from life. Depression is not a character flaw — it is a clinical reality, and it responds to the right kind of support.

"I wanted to help myself first. And when I did, helping others became something I had to do."

Online sessions · Arabic & English
About Nisreen

A psychologist who
started by healing herself

I didn't come to psychology from a textbook. I came from my own story — the moments where I had to sit with difficult feelings and find my way through them. That personal path is what made me want to become the person I once needed: someone who truly shows up.

My training has given me the clinical foundation. But what I bring to every session goes beyond that. I bring real presence. I listen in a way that makes you feel felt, not analysed. I ask questions that open things up rather than shut them down. And I never rush you toward an answer you're not ready for.

I believe therapy is not about fixing what's wrong with you. There is nothing wrong with you. It's about understanding yourself deeply enough to stop being at war with who you are — and finding your way back to peace.

Sessions in English Sessions in Arabic
Sessions & Offers

How we can work together

All sessions are 1 hour, held online via video or voice call. Whether you're starting for the first time or continuing a journey — there's a place for you here.

Single Session

First Step Session

Not ready to commit to ongoing therapy? Start with one focused hour. We talk about what's on your mind, I help you see it more clearly, and you leave with something useful — even if we never meet again.

1 hour · Online · Video or voice Arabic or English No prior therapy experience needed
Package · Most Popular

4-Session Package

Real change needs real time. This package gives us four sessions to go beneath the surface — to understand your patterns, work through what's holding you back, and build lasting tools for your mind.

4 × 1 hour · Weekly or bi-weekly Structured but flexible to your needs Ongoing support between sessions
Recommended for most clients
Package · Deep Work

8-Session Package

For those ready to do the deeper work — healing attachment wounds, understanding codependency, rebuilding self-worth, or processing grief. Eight sessions to genuinely transform how you relate to yourself.

8 × 1 hour · Your pace Deep psychological exploration Personalised exercises between sessions
Speciality

Anxiety & Emotional Regulation

Tailored sessions focused on understanding your anxiety from the root — not just managing symptoms. You'll learn to recognise your triggers, calm your nervous system, and feel safe inside your own body again.

Available as single or package Practical tools you can use daily
Speciality

Relationship & Attachment Work

Exploring why you love the way you love — patterns of codependency, over-giving, fear of abandonment, or emotional unavailability. This work changes how you show up in every relationship, including with yourself.

Recommended as 4–8 session package Evidence-informed approach
Speciality

Creative Psychology Sessions

Psychology doesn't have to feel heavy. These sessions use writing prompts, reflective exercises, and creative tools alongside clinical methods — because sometimes insight arrives through a sentence, not an analysis.

Unique approach, not available everywhere Especially helpful for writers & thinkers
Clinical Approach

Cognitive Behavioural Therapy (CBT)

CBT helps you understand the connection between your thoughts, feelings, and behaviour. Together we identify the thinking patterns that are keeping you stuck — and replace them with ones that actually serve you. Structured, practical, and rooted in evidence.

Evidence-based approach Practical tools you use between sessions Effective for anxiety, depression & overthinking
Clinical Approach

Logo Therapy

Developed by Viktor Frankl, Logo Therapy centres on one question: what gives your life meaning? When nothing makes sense and pain feels pointless, this approach helps you find purpose even inside the difficulty. It's not about eliminating suffering — it's about choosing who you become through it.

Meaning-centred psychotherapy Especially powerful in grief & existential pain Available as single or package sessions

Not sure which option is right for you? Reach out and we'll figure it out together.

Get in Touch
My Belief

Therapy is about
never leaving you
alone.

Even when you've left yourself alone — avoided your feelings, pushed through, numbed out — I won't do that. I stay. I look. I ask the questions no one has asked you before. And together, we find the way through.

Therapy is about never letting someone be alone in their hardest moments. Even if that someone is you — with yourself. — Nisreen Burhan

How to hear your inner world, understand your triggers, and stop running from what you feel
By Nisreen Burhan · Clinical Psychology
Free Ebook

Something to start
your journey with

Inside you, there is a voice that has been knocking for a long time. It speaks through your reactions, your body, your patterns. Most of us have learned to silence it — or never learned to hear it at all.

This free ebook will help you begin. Understand your triggers. Recognise what your emotions are really saying. And take the first step toward the peace that's already waiting for you.

No spam. Just one meaningful resource, from me to you.

Things I think about

I write the way I talk in sessions — honestly, without performing. These essays are about the inner life: the patterns we carry, the feelings we avoid, and the quiet work of becoming more yourself.
